title: Natural-orbital locking reveals hidden steady-state skin order in Gaussian open fermion chains
authors: Y. T. Wang, X. Z. Zhang
abstract: Nonreciprocal relaxation matrices can have skin-localized right eigenmodes, but their imprint on a mixed steady state is not fixed by the density profile alone. We develop an exact steady-state theory for number-conserving Gaussian fermion chains and show that the dominant natural orbital of the correlation matrix provides a mode-resolved diagnostic of hidden skin order. The steady-state correlator admits a biorthogonal decomposition in terms of the left and right eigenmodes of the relaxation matrix $X$ and the source matrix $Y$. This formula separates three ingredients: slow rapidity denominators, source loading by left eigenmodes, and real-space geometry from right eigenmodes. For a local pump, the pump position is read by the left modes, whereas the selected profile is drawn by the right modes. In a single-slow-mode regime, the dominant natural orbital locks to the Euclidean-normalized slow right mode. The density can follow the same boundary trend, but it is a less selective incoherent sum over occupied natural orbitals. We verify this selection law in a nonreciprocal Hatano--Nelson chain and show that, in a nonreciprocal SSH chain, the selected natural orbital crosses over from a topological edge candidate to a slow bulk-skin candidate. These results identify natural-orbital locking as a steady-state diagnostic of nonreciprocal localization in Gaussian open fermion chains.
summary: The paper investigates how nonreciprocal skin effects manifest in the steady state of open fermion chains. It introduces 'natural-orbital locking' as a superior diagnostic compared to traditional density profiles for identifying hidden skin order. By analyzing the biorthogonal decomposition of the correlation matrix, the authors show that the dominant natural orbital tracks the slow right eigenmode of the relaxation matrix. They demonstrate this effect in both Hatano-Nelson and SSH models, specifically identifying a crossover between topological edge-state and bulk-skin-state localization.
main_problem: Identifying a direct steady-state observable to detect hidden nonreciprocal skin order in Lindbladian systems and determining if natural orbitals provide a sharper diagnostic than local density profiles.
main_result: The authors demonstrate 'natural-orbital locking,' where the dominant natural orbital of the correlation matrix locks to the slow right eigenmode, providing a mode-resolved diagnostic of skin order and revealing a crossover between topological edge-locking and bulk-skin-locking.
method: The study uses an exact steady-state theory for Gaussian fermion chains based on a biorthogonal decomposition of the steady-state correlator into left and right eigenmodes of the relaxation matrix.
model_or_system: Number-conserving Gaussian open fermion chains, specifically including nonreciprocal Hatano-Nelson and nonreciprocal SSH models, governed by quadratic Lindbladian dynamics.
key_observables: Dominant natural orbital (phi_max), local density profile, source-loading factor, and overlaps with edge and slow bulk-skin modes.
important_parameters: Nonreciprocity parameter (g), damping/loss strength (kappa), pump strength (gamma), and the single-slow-mode regime.
main_assumptions: The dynamics are assumed to be quadratic and number-conserving; certain local decompositions require specific positivity conditions on the loss and pump matrices.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the conceptual comparison between density and natural orbitals; Figure 2 validates the selection law in Hatano-Nelson chains; Figure 3 illustrates the crossover between edge and bulk-skin locking in SSH chains.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of hidden skin order, develops an exact steady-state theory via biorthogonal decomposition, applies the theory to the Hatano-Nelson model to validate the selection law, and then explores the crossover regime in nonreciprocal SSH chains.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a new, sharper diagnostic tool (natural-orbital locking) for characterizing non-Hermitian skin effects in dissipative many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conceptual definition of hidden steady-state skin or- der. A finite Gaussian open chain is driven by a local pump Y = Γ |s⟩⟨s| and relaxes under a non-Hermitian matrix X. The exact steady-state kernel can be written as the integral in Eq. (10) or as the biorthogonal expansion in Eq. (13). Diagonalizing Css produces natural orbitals. The schematic comparison on the right illustrates the diagnostic used be- low. The Euclidean-normalized slow right mode | bRslow(j)|2

FIG. 2. Hatano–Nelson chain and validation of the source-selection law. The relaxation matrix is Eq. (21), and all panels use N = 40, tR = 1, tL = 0.17, κ = 0.91, Γ = 0.03, s∗= 15. (a) Euclidean-normalized slow right-mode profile | bRslow(j)|2, dominant natural-orbital profile |φmax(j)|2, and normalized density ˜nj for a pump at s∗= 15. The natural orbital tracks the selected right mode directly, while the density is the diagonal contraction of the same steady-state mixture. (b) Source-position scan comparing the normalized exact loading νmax(s)/ maxs′ νmax(s′) with the normalized analytic prediction A1(s)/ maxs′ A1(s′). The source dependence is fixed by the left-mode weight |L1(s)|2. (c)...

FIG. 3. Nonreciprocal SSH chain and crossover between edge locking and bulk-skin locking. The target matrix is Eq. (27), the common parameters are N = 20, t1 = 0.50, t2 = 1.00, κ = 1.50, Γ = 10−8, (m, A) = (1, A), and the overlaps are defined in Eq. (28). (a) Crossover of Oedge and Oslow as a function of nonreciprocity g. The dotted lines mark gedge = −0.25 and gbulk = 0.20. (b) At gedge, the dominant natural orbital tracks the Euclidean-normalized edge candidate | bRedge(j)|2. (c) At gbulk, it tracks the Euclidean-normalized slow bulk-skin mode | bRslow(j)|2. In both panels the normalized density ˜nj is shown for comparison. Panels (b) and (c) use different vertical scales.
